reasoning: Butterfly is a natural kind representing a specific order of insects. It is essential to all its instances across all possible worlds - something cannot be a butterfly in one world and not in another, thus it is rigid (+R). As a biological kind, it carries and supplies its own identity condition based on biological and genetic criteria (+I, +O). Butterflies are intrinsic wholes with biological unity - they have integrated parts forming a complete organism (+U). They do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist in the sense of being a substance in themselves, but their lifecycle does depend on specific environmental conditions and hosts (-D).

"Red" is a color property that can be attributed to various objects. It is not essential for any object to be red across all possible worlds; objects can change color or not have color at all, making "Red" non-rigid (-R). The property does not carry an inherent identity condition (+I) as it does not define what an object is but rather how it appears. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) since being red is a qualitative property that does not distinguish one object from another based on its essence. "Red" does not unify anything into a whole; it is a property that can be attributed to parts or wholes alike (-U). The property of being red does not depend on anything else to exist; it is an independent attribute that can be applied or removed without affecting the existence of the object it describes (-D).

"Food" represents a category of consumable substances. Analyzing its meta-properties: It is non-rigid (-R) because something that is food in one context or culture may not be considered food in another. It carries identity (+I) as there are general criteria (nutritional, consumable, etc.) that distinguish food from non-food items. However, it does not supply its own identity condition (-O) since the identity of specific food items (like apples or bread) is given by their own kinds, not by the general category of food. Food items can be considered as intrinsic wholes (+U) when referring to specific types of food, but the category itself is somewhat abstract. Food does not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist in the sense that it does not require a specific other entity to be what it is (-D).

The term "Animal" refers to a broad category of living organisms characterized by their ability to move, feed, and respond to stimuli. Analyzing its meta-properties, we find that being an animal is essential to all instances across all possible worlds, as something cannot be an animal in one context and not in another. This suggests rigidity. Animals also carry an identity condition based on biological and genetic characteristics, and this identity is intrinsic to what it means to be an animal. Considering unity, animals are generally considered to be unified wholes with integrated parts forming a complete organism. Lastly, animals do not depend on other entities to exist in the same way that, for example, a role or a phase might, as they are substances in themselves.

The term "RedApple" represents a specific type of apple characterized by its red color. Analyzing its ontological meta-properties, we find that being a RedApple is not essential to all instances of apples, as an apple can change color or not be red and still be an apple. This suggests that RedApple is non-rigid. It carries an identity condition inherited from the broader category of apples but does not supply its own unique identity condition beyond the color attribute. RedApples are unified wholes with physical boundaries. They do not depend on other entities to exist beyond the normal dependencies of physical objects.

A "Group" can refer to a collection of entities that share certain characteristics or are related in some way. Analyzing its meta-properties: It is non-rigid (-R) because being a group is not essential to the individual members - they can exist independently of the group. The property does not carry a clear identity condition (-I) as groups can be highly variable and context-dependent. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) since the identity of a group often depends on the nature of its members or the context in which it is considered. Groups can exhibit either unity or lack thereof, depending on their nature (e.g., a sports team vs. a random assembly of people), but generally, they are not intrinsic wholes in the same sense as natural kinds (~U). A group depends on its members to exist (+D), as without members, there is no group.

The term "Country" refers to a geopolitical entity with defined boundaries, sovereignty, and a unified government. Analyzing its ontological meta-properties: A country is essentially a country across all possible worlds where it exists (+R), as its identity as a sovereign state does not change. It carries an identity condition (+I) since being a country implies a set of characteristics that differentiate it from other entities. Furthermore, it supplies its own identity condition (+O) based on its sovereignty, territorial boundaries, and governance structure. A country is considered an intrinsic whole (+U) as it encompasses a defined territory and population under a single governance. Lastly, a country's existence does not intrinsically depend on other entities (-D), as it is defined by its sovereignty and independence.

"AmountOfMatter" refers to a quantity or measure of material substance. Analyzing its meta-properties: It is anti-rigid (~R) because the amount of matter can change without the entity (the matter itself) ceasing to exist - matter can be added or removed, changing the amount but not the essence of being matter. It does not carry identity (-I) as the amount of matter does not provide a condition for identifying specific instances of matter over time. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) since the identity of matter is based on its inherent properties, not the quantity. The unity of amount of matter is non-unifying (-U) because it represents a quantity that can be divided or aggregated without affecting the intrinsic nature of the matter itself. Amount of matter is dependent (+D) on the existence of matter, as it is a property or attribute of matter, not a standalone entity.

"PhysicalObject" represents entities with physical presence and spatial boundedness. Analyzing its meta-properties: It is rigid (+R) because being a physical object is essential to all instances of physical objects across all possible worlds - something cannot be a physical object in one world and not in another. It carries identity (+I) as physical objects have identity conditions based on spatiotemporal continuity and material constitution. However, it does not supply its own specific identity condition beyond general physical existence (-O), as specific identities within this category (e.g., chair, car) are determined by more specific properties. Physical objects typically form intrinsic wholes with defined physical boundaries (+U). They do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist in the sense of their physical presence (-D).

"GroupOfPeople" is a collective entity representing multiple individuals. Analyzing its meta-properties: It is non-rigid (-R) because the membership of a group of people can change over time without the group ceasing to exist as a group - people can join or leave. It does not carry a strong identity condition (-I) as the identity of the group is not fixed and can vary based on its members or purpose. Consequently, it does not supply its own identity condition (-O). A group of people does not form an intrinsic whole in the same way a single person does; it is more about the collection or aggregation of individuals (~U). A group of people is dependent on the existence of its members (+D); without people, there cannot be a group.

"LivingBeing" represents a broad category of organisms that exhibit the characteristics of life. Analyzing its meta-properties: It is rigid (+R) because being a living being is an essential property that applies to all its instances across all possible worlds - an entity cannot be a living being in one world and not in another. It carries identity (+I) as living beings have clear biological and physiological identity conditions. It supplies its own identity condition (+O) as the criteria for being a living being (e.g., metabolism, reproduction) serve to distinguish living entities from non-living ones. Living beings are intrinsic wholes with integrated biological systems (+U). However, living beings often depend on their environment and other living or non-living entities for survival (+D), such as dependence on air, water, and nutrients.

"SocialEntity" refers to entities that exist due to social conventions, agreements, or interactions, such as corporations, marriages, or governments. These entities are not rigid (+R) because their existence and nature can vary significantly across different social contexts and possible worlds. However, for the sake of this analysis and considering the broader interpretation of social entities as being defined by their social roles and functions, we might consider them as having a certain level of rigidity in the context of the social structures they inhabit. They carry identity (+I) as social entities have specific roles, functions, or purposes that define them within a society. However, they typically do not supply their own identity conditions (-O) since their identities are often derived from the social context, agreements, or legal frameworks that establish them. Social entities can exhibit unity (+U) in the sense that they often have defined structures, roles, and boundaries within the social sphere. Nonetheless, they are dependent (+D) on the society, legal system, or collective agreement that recognizes and sustains them.

"Location" represents a spatial or geographical point, area, or region where something exists or can be found. It is not essential for all instances in all possible worlds to have the same location - locations can change, making it non-rigid (-R). A location carries an identity condition (+I) as it can be uniquely identified by its spatial coordinates or geographical boundaries. However, it does not supply its own identity condition in the sense that the identity of a location is often derived from its relation to other entities or spatial references (-O). Locations themselves are not intrinsic wholes but rather are defined by their boundaries or the space they occupy, which can be considered as non-unifying (-U) since they do not have inherent unity like objects but are rather a spatial context. Locations are independent in the sense that they exist regardless of the presence of other entities (-D).

"Fruit" is a natural kind representing the ripe ovary of plants, including seeds, typically sweet and fleshy. It is essential to all its instances across all possible worlds - something cannot be a fruit in one world and not in another (rigid). As a biological kind, it carries and supplies its own identity condition based on botanical criteria (own identity). Fruits are intrinsic wholes with biological unity - they have integrated parts forming a complete organism (unifying). Fruits do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist beyond normal botanical or environmental conditions - they are substances in themselves (independent).

"Vertebrate" is a biological classification that represents animals with backbones. It is rigid (+R) because having a backbone is an essential characteristic that defines vertebrates across all possible worlds - something cannot be a vertebrate without a backbone. This property carries identity (+I) as the presence or absence of a backbone is a critical criterion for distinguishing vertebrates from other animals. It supplies its own identity condition (+O) because being a vertebrate directly implies the presence of a backbone, which serves as a defining characteristic. Vertebrates are intrinsic wholes (+U) as they are organisms with integrated parts. They do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist beyond normal ecological interactions (-D).

"Caterpillar" refers to the larval stage of a butterfly or moth. Analyzing its meta-properties: It is anti-rigid (~R) because being a caterpillar is a phase in the life cycle of certain insects, and an individual that is a caterpillar will eventually become something else (a pupa and then a butterfly or moth), thus it is essential not to be essential. It carries identity (+I) as being a caterpillar distinguishes it from other stages of its life cycle and other organisms. However, it does not supply its own identity condition since its identity is part of a broader life cycle (-O). A caterpillar is an intrinsic whole with its own biological unity (+U). It is dependent (+D) because its existence as a caterpillar is intrinsically tied to its development into later stages of its life cycle.

"Person" represents a human being, characterized by consciousness, rationality, and a complex biological and psychological makeup. Analyzing its meta-properties: It is rigid (+R) because being a person is essential to all instances across all possible worlds - a person remains a person regardless of external changes. It carries identity (+I) as persons have intrinsic identity conditions based on biological, psychological, and social continuity. It supplies its own identity condition (+O) since being a person is what defines an entity's identity in this context. Persons are intrinsic wholes with integrated physical and psychological parts (+U). However, persons are often considered to be dependent on their biological, social, or relational contexts for their existence or identity (+D), although this can be debated.

"LegalAgent" refers to an entity capable of taking legal actions, which can be a person, organization, or even an artificial entity granted legal personhood. This concept is anti-rigid (~R) because being a legal agent is not an essential property of any entity across all possible worlds; entities can gain or lose this status based on legal definitions and recognitions. It carries identity (+I) as legal agents are recognized and differentiated based on legal criteria. However, the identity condition is not supplied by the property of being a legal agent itself but by the entity's underlying nature (e.g., a person's identity or an organization's founding documents), so it does not supply its own identity condition (-O). Legal agents can be seen as unifying (+U) in the sense that they represent a cohesivelegal entity capable of acting within a legal framework. The existence of a legal agent is dependent (+D) on the legal system recognizing it as such, making it dependent on external legal definitions and frameworks.

"Entity" is a very general term that encompasses anything that exists, including objects, concepts, and relations. It is essential to all its instances across all possible worlds in the sense that something cannot be an entity in one world and not in another if it indeed exists (-R is less applicable as Entity itself doesn't change, but its instances can, making the analysis nuanced). As a fundamental concept, it carries an identity condition in a broad sense, as it distinguishes existing things from non-existing ones (+I). However, it does not supply its own identity condition in a specific sense, as the identity of an entity depends on what kind of entity it is (-O). Entities can be either unifying (if considering individual objects) or non-unifying (if considering abstract concepts or relations), but given its broad nature, it leans towards being non-unifying (-U) as it encompasses a wide range of existents. Entities, by definition, do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist in a general sense, though specific entities may have dependencies (-D).

"Organization" refers to a social entity with a collective structure and purpose. It is not essential for something to be an organization in all possible worlds, as entities can change their organizational structure or dissolve, indicating non-rigidity (-R). The concept of an organization carries an identity condition based on its structure, goals, and membership (+I), but the identity condition is not supplied by the property of being an organization itself, as organizations are often defined by their specific roles, functions, or types (-O). Organizations can be considered as unified wholes with coordinated parts (+U), but they often intrinsically depend on their members, resources, or other organizations to exist and function, indicating dependence (+D).

"Agent" is a role or function that an entity can play, implying the capability to act or perform tasks. It is anti-rigid (~R) because being an agent is not an essential property of any entity across all possible worlds - entities can take on or lose the role of being an agent. The property does not carry an inherent identity condition (-I) since what makes something an agent is its ability to act, not an intrinsic identity. It does not supply its own identity condition (-O) as the identity of an agent is derived from the entity performing the role. Agents can be considered unifying (+U) in the sense that they integrate various capabilities to perform actions, but this unity is functional rather than intrinsic. Agents are dependent (+D) on the entities that embody them, as the role of an agent is typically played by another entity with the capacity for action.

"Apple" is a natural kind representing a type of fruit. It is essential to all its instances across all possible worlds - something cannot be an apple in one world and not in another, making it rigid (+R). As a natural kind, it carries and supplies its own identity condition based on biological and genetic criteria (+I, +O). Apples are intrinsic wholes with biological unity - they have integrated parts forming a complete fruit (+U). Apples do not intrinsically depend on other entities to exist beyond normal environmental conditions - they are substances in themselves (-D).
